narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the admissions model predicted a 64% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which led to the rejection of your law school application. Your profile shows strong undergraduate performance with a 3.60 GPA, well above the typical 3.21 average, along with plans to attend full-time and a middle-class family income background. However, your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1—far below the average of about 5.7—and your Year 3 decile of 1, also much lower than the usual 5.5, stood out as the most influential factors in this decision. These early low rankings signaled potential struggles with consistent academic performance, strongly pushing the model's prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 27, significantly below the dataset average of 36.7, which further reinforced the model's concern about your readiness for the rigors ahead. Your family income bracket came after that, with its middle level not providing the same boost as higher incomes often do in similar profiles. Your racial background as Black, underrepresented at just 4.6% compared to 83.8% White students, also played a notable role in tilting the prediction negatively, followed by your solid UGPA, full-time status, and gender as female—which had the least impact overall. In ranking their influence, Year 1 decile topped the list, closely followed by Year 3 decile, then LSAT, family income, race, UGPA, full-time plans, and finally gender—all contributing in that order to push toward a failure prediction rather than success. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early deciles and LSAT were notably weaker, while your UGPA was a relative strength but not enough to outweigh the rest. To improve your chances next time, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above 36, as that's a changeable factor with dedicated prep. Building a stronger academic trajectory through additional coursework or certifications could help offset those low early deciles. Seeking bar exam prep resources tailored for underrepresented students, like targeted tutoring or study programs, might also demonstrate your commitment and shift future predictions in your favor. Keep pushing forward—you have a solid UGPA foundation to build on.
